proposals.old_exact:
[¶10] We do "not render advisory opinions, and an appeal will be dismissed if the issues become moot or academic, leaving no actual controversy to be determined." Schwartzenberger v. McKenzie Cnty. Bd. of Cnty. Comm'rs, 2017 ND 211, ¶ 6, 901 N.W.2d 64. This appeal presents a request for an advisory opinion. We conclude the appeal is moot and therefore dismiss the appeal.

[¶11] The appeal is dismissed.

[¶12] Jon J. Jensen, C.J. Daniel J. Crothers Lisa Fair McEvers Jerod E. Tufte Douglas A. Bahr

III

proposals.old_exact:
[¶8] Prior to the hearing, the parties were notified about the length of the hearing. In Mairs v. Mairs, 2014 ND 132, ¶ 12, 847 N.W.2d 785, this Court concluded the district court did not violate due process by imposing a three-hour per party limitation on a hearing to change primary residential responsibility and amend parenting time. Similarly here, the court notified the parties of the time allotted for the evidentiary hearing and provided each with two and one half hours to present their cases. The court allowed closing arguments to be submitted by brief to accommodate the reduced time. The time per side permitted an opportunity for Bibler and Zittleman to examine and cross-examine witnesses, albeit not as much time as Bibler now claims to have wanted. On this record, we conclude the court did not violate Bibler's due process rights by limiting the evidentiary hearing to five hours and allocating half of that time to each party. In addition to the due process claim, we consider whether the district court

[¶9] abused its discretion by reducing the length of the evidentiary hearing.
proposals.new_exact:
[¶8] Prior to the hearing, the parties were notified about the length of the hearing. In Mairs v. Mairs, 2014 ND 132, ¶ 12, 847 N.W.2d 785, this Court concluded the district court did not violate due process by imposing a three-hour per party limitation on a hearing to change primary residential responsibility and amend parenting time. Similarly here, the court notified the parties of the time allotted for the evidentiary hearing and provided each with two and one half hours to present their cases. The court allowed closing arguments to be submitted by brief to accommodate the reduced time. The time per side permitted an opportunity for Bibler and Zittleman to examine and cross-examine witnesses, albeit not as much time as Bibler now claims to have wanted. On this record, we conclude the court did not violate Bibler's due process rights by limiting the evidentiary hearing to five hours and allocating half of that time to each party.

[¶9] In addition to the due process claim, we consider whether the district court abused its discretion by reducing the length of the evidentiary hearing.
proposals.evidence: PDF shows [¶9] beginning with 'In addition to the due process claim...' as a complete paragraph. DB incorrectly splits this sentence: the first part ('In addition...consider whether the district court') is appended to the end of [¶8], and only the second part ('abused its discretion...') begins [¶9]. This is a paragraph sequencing/structural error during OCR/ingestion.
proposals.note: The sentence spanning [¶8]-[¶9] in the DB appears to have been incorrectly parsed during PDF text extraction, with the paragraph boundary placed in the middle of a sentence rather than after the period ending [¶8].

coverage_note: Full opinion read and compared. One structural error found (paragraph_seq class). All other content verified as matching the PDF source except for expected page furniture (page numbers, form feeds) which are handled by a separate deterministic pass."
